pattern: Four worker phases (crossing over, picking up, crossing back, putting down) all run in parallel except for one shared single-lane bridge, so the whole problem reduces to repeatedly handing the bridge to whichever ready worker has the lowest crossing priority and jumping the clock straight to the next state change.
hints: Model this as a discrete-event simulation rather than a minute-by-minute loop: each worker is always in exactly one of five phases (idle on the far side, crossing over, handling the item, crossing back, or handling it on the near side), and only the crossing phases ever contend with each other.

When the bridge is free, a worker already on the far side waiting to cross back always wins over a worker on the near side waiting to cross over; within either group, rank candidates by their own total crossing cost (both directions combined), and break ties by treating the larger index as the lower priority. Also stop sending new workers over once every remaining item already has a worker assigned to fetch it.

Keep four small priority structures: near-side idle workers, far-side workers ready to cross back, workers still mid-pickup, and workers still mid-putdown. At each step either resolve one crossing at the current clock time (advancing the clock by that crossing's duration), or, if nobody can cross right now, jump the clock forward to whichever pickup/putdown finishes soonest; record the clock time every time a crossing-back finishes, and return the last one once every item has arrived.

edge_inputs.description: three workers with increasing round-trip cost share a single item, exercising the base crossing-priority order.
one much faster worker and one much slower worker split three items, overlapping pickups/putdowns with bridge crossings.
a single worker and a single item, the smallest possible workforce and workload.
every worker has identical crossing and handling times, so only the index tie-break can decide who goes first.
one clearly efficient worker among two far slower ones, so the efficiency ranking must dominate the dispatch order.
two workers whose combined crossing time ties exactly, so the tie-break must treat the higher index as lower priority.
a lone worker must ferry every item by itself, forcing one fully sequential chain of round trips.
far more workers than items, so idle workers must stop being sent over once every item is already claimed.

stress.description: a moderate random workforce and workload spanning the full allowed timing range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    k = max(1, min(50, max(1, size // 4)))
    time = [[rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(4)] for _ in range(k)]
    return [n, k, time]

stress.description: a smaller workforce built from repeated (and lightly perturbed) timing rows, to stress the efficiency tie-break
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 60))
    k = max(1, min(10, max(1, size // 4)))
    base = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(4)]
    time = []
    for _ in range(k):
        row = base[:]
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            idx = rng.randrange(4)
            row[idx] = rng.randint(1, 50)
        time.append(row)
    return [n, k, time]
